title: Fixing Missing Left Child Node Handling in a Binary Tree

statement.markdown: Given the root of a binary tree, you need to return the inorder traverse of the tree in a list. However, your current implementation fails to handle the case where the given tree has no left child.
statement.inputFormat: The input is a string representation of a binary tree, where each non-empty node is represented as 'X' and the empty nodes are represented as '.'
statement.outputFormat: The function should return a list containing the inorder traverse of the binary tree.
statement.notes: The time complexity of the function should be O(n), where n is the number of nodes in the tree.

examples.output: The output should be a list containing the inorder traverse of the binary tree
examples.explanation: For the given binary tree 3,[9,3,20,15,7], the inorder traverse is [9, 3, 15, 20, 7]

hints.text: Check the implementation of your recursive function. Is it correctly handling the case where the given tree has no left child?

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The inorder traverse of a binary tree can be performed using a recursive function that visits the left subtree, then the node, and finally the right subtree.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach can be used to solve this problem but it would be inefficient for large trees.
editorial.betterSolution: We can solve this problem more efficiently by using a recursive function to traverse the tree.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can solve this problem in O(n) time complexity by using a recursive function to traverse the tree.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by analyzing the time and space complexity and ensuring that the solution handles all cases correctly.
editorial.dryRun: For the given binary tree 3,[9,3,20,15,7], the inorder traverse is [9, 3, 15, 20, 7]
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the given tree has no left child.
Using an inefficient algorithm to solve the problem.

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<int> inorderTraversal(TreeNode* root); };
starterCode.java: class Solution { public: List<Integer> inorderTraversal(TreeNode root); }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def inorderTraversal(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]:

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: vector<int> inorderTraversal(TreeNode* root) { if (root) { vector<int> left = inorderTraversal(root->left); return left; } } };
buggyStarterCode.java: class Solution { public List<Integer> inorderTraversal(TreeNode root) { if (root != null) { List<Integer> left = inorderTraversal(root.left); return left; } } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def inorderTraversal(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]: if root: return self.inorderTraversal(root.left)
buggyStarterCode.javascript: class Solution { inorderTraversal(root) { if (root) { return this.inorderTraversal(root.left); } } }

solutions.python:
class Solution: def inorderTraversal(self, root: TreeNode) -> List[int]: res = [] if root: res = self.inorderTraversal(root.left) res.append(root.val) res = res + self.inorderTraversal(root.right) return res


# Testcase 1:
root = TreeNode(3)
root.left = TreeNode(9)
root.right = TreeNode(20)
root.right.left = TreeNode(15)
root.right.right = TreeNode(7)
print(Solution().inorderTraversal(root))  # Output: [9, 3, 15, 20, 7]

# Testcase 2:
# The binary tree is:
#      1
#     / \
#    2   3
#   / \
#  4   5
root = TreeNode(1)
root.left = TreeNode(2)
root.right = TreeNode(3)
root.left.left = TreeNode(4)
root.left.right = TreeNode(5)
print(Solution().inorderTraversal(root)) # Output: [4, 2, 5, 1, 3]
solutions.cpp:
class Solution { public: vector<int> inorderTraversal(TreeNode* root) { vector<int> res; if (root) { if (root->left) res = inorderTraversal(root->left); res.push_back(root->val); if (root->right) res = res + inorderTraversal(root->right); } return res; } }; int main() { TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(3); root->left = new TreeNode(9); root->right = new TreeNode(20); root->right->left = new TreeNode(15); root->right->right = new TreeNode(7); for (auto x : Solution().inorderTraversal(root)) { cout << x << ' '; } cout << '
'; return 0; }
